items.title: EIA flags the Strait of Hormuz as a critical global oil chokepoint
items.description: EIA’s June 2025 analysis explains why the Strait of Hormuz matters for world oil flows, shipping risk, and price volatility.
items.impact: This is the clearest recent reminder that Malaysia’s fuel outlook still sits inside a global market, not a closed domestic bubble.

items.title: MOF says e-hailing BUDI95 tiering will reach up to 800 litres for the highest-distance users
items.description: MOF explained that e-hailing drivers can qualify for tiered additional BUDI95 eligibility, with the highest band reaching 800 litres per month.
items.impact: This is the clearest official example of the subsidy system moving from blanket support to targeted, category-based support.

items.title: MOF cut unsubsidised RON97 and RON95 at year-end while subsidised RON95 stayed at RM1.99
items.description: The late-December weekly update lowered unsubsidised RON97 and RON95, kept subsidised RON95 at RM1.99, and left Sabah, Sarawak and Labuan diesel at RM2.15.
items.impact: This shows the policy split between market-priced fuel and the targeted BUDI95 layer the site keeps explaining.

items.title: MOF’s March retail-price release raised RON97, non-subsidised RON95, and Peninsular diesel
items.description: The official retail-price release set RON97 at RM3.25, non-subsidised RON95 at RM2.67, and Peninsular diesel at RM3.12 from 5 to 11 March 2026.
items.impact: This is one of the clearest examples of how weekly price updates track global oil movement with a short lag.

items.title: RON97 fell to RM4.95 while Peninsular diesel rose to RM6.02
items.description: MOF’s April 1 citation confirmed a weekly retail-price change that lowered RON97 and increased Peninsular diesel for 2 to 8 April 2026.
items.impact: It shows how different fuel lines can move in opposite directions even within the same week.

items.title: MOF held subsidised fuel prices at RM1.99 and kept East Malaysia diesel at RM2.15
items.description: MOF said the weekly average-cost formula means current pump prices still reflect earlier high supply costs even after the global market eased.
items.impact: This is the strongest recent explanation of the gap between headline oil prices and the price people actually see at the pump.
